title: Internal and external sources of finance
summary: 9609 AS — internal and external sources, advantages, disadvantages, and matching to situations.
learningObjectives: List and explain internal sources of finance.
List and explain external sources of finance.
Compare advantages and disadvantages of each.
Match appropriate sources to business situations.

simpleExplanation.title: Inside or outside the business
simpleExplanation.summary: Internal funds come from within the business; external funds come from outside owners, lenders, or investors.
steps: Internal: retained profit, sale of assets, working capital reduction.
External: shares, loans, overdraft, leasing, trade credit.
Leasing and trade credit are external despite day-to-day use.
Crowdfunding and venture capital for start-ups.

flashcards.back: Retained profit, sale of redundant assets, reducing inventory, tighter credit control.
Share issue, bank loan, overdraft, debentures, leasing, trade credit, crowdfunding, venture capital.
No interest, no dilution, no repayment schedule — if profits exist.
Dilutes ownership and control; flotation costs for PLC.
Overdraft: flexible short-term facility. Loan: fixed amount, fixed term — for planned investment.
External — acquire asset without large upfront cost; no ownership (operating lease).
Pay suppliers later — free short-term finance if paid within discount period.
External equity for high-growth start-ups — investors expect high returns and often influence.

sections.content: **Sources of finance** (5.2.2) is one of the **most examined AS topics**. Build a revision table: **Source | Internal/External | Best for | Pros | Cons**. Examiners ask you to **match** a scenario (start-up, expansion, cash crisis) to the right source.

**Retained profit** — profit kept in the business rather than paid as dividends. Main source for established profitable firms.

**Sale of assets** — sell unused property, equipment, or investments.

**Improve working capital management** — reduce inventory, collect receivables faster (releases cash without external finance).

items: **Share capital** — new shares to existing or new investors (Ltd/PLC).
**Bank loan** — medium/long-term fixed borrowing for assets.
**Overdraft** — short-term flexible borrowing for cash gaps.
**Leasing** — pay to use equipment; preserves cash.
**Trade credit** — suppliers allow payment after delivery.
**Crowdfunding / venture capital** — start-ups and innovative projects.

sections.question:
Match each need to the most suitable source:
(a) Replace delivery van — $40 000, 5-year life.
(b) Cover wages for 2 weeks when customers pay late.
(c) Fund R&D for unprofitable start-up.
sections.solution:
(a) **Bank loan** or **leasing** — fixed asset, medium term; leasing avoids large upfront cash.

(b) **Overdraft** — short-term **working capital** gap; flexible and quick.

(c) **Venture capital** or **crowdfunding** — no retained profit; high risk needs **equity investors** willing to accept no immediate return.

sections.content: Never recommend **share issue** for a **sole trader** without first mentioning **incorporation**. Never use **retained profit** if the case says the firm made a **loss**.

sections.label: Mark a sources of finance question
